video_title: Would a Capuchin Monkey Make a Good Pet? | Pets Rated 😲 #capuchinmonkey #animals
video_description: Smart, curious, and incredibly social—capuchin monkeys can be adorable and challenging at the same time. Could they make a ...
text_content:
Unfortunately, like many NHP’s, Capuchins are marketed to many as kids that don’t grow up. Here’s the thing; all animals grow up and mature. And these guys want some independence and companionship.
They’re toddlers with knives for teeth so be prepared for that.
Not to mention how they’re acquired. Primates all have the planet’s longest time with their mothers before they’re mature, at least 3-4 years. Taking them away, which when poached will often result in the mother’s death and those of the troop members, causes a lot of psychological issues. Unfortunately, baby monkeys are the main marketable ones on social media so there’s a lot of money to just keep these monkeys for a few years in some countries and then, when they’re too big to be cute, either release them into the wild with no experience or.., something truly soul destroying.
In countries they’re not native to, they’re more expensive so people do keep them. The laws do vary though. Come next year and the rules for owning one will be heavily raised in the UK.

Did I say one? That’s the other thing about all NHP’s aside from maybe orangutans where the males are very solitary; they are incredibly social animals that, in the wild, spend their days roaming and foraging. You can’t just have one or two. They need a group. And, as for space, they need more than we can ever give them.
That giving them to sanctuaries part does forget one other thing; in countries like the US and UK, the sanctuaries are pretty much full because there’s no real naturally occurring wild population in the countries to rehabilitate them to live in. They’re essentially retirement homes for creatures that, usually given up around the age of 7, will be stuck there for about 30 years if they have a space. Many of which may not even know how to interact with their own species in subjects like troop dynamics because they were taken from their mothers before they could learn.

Sorry for the rant. Just wanted to give some extra information about these little guys because poaching and the wild animal trade are pretty foul.

Now, I know the main argument in the US will be that some have probably seen a Capuchin be an animal assistant for severely disabled people. Whilst that was true, they’re no longer listed as that category of animal for many reasons and the charity now looks after them all full time in their retirement, now looking towards robotics instead.

video_title: Would a Capuchin Monkey Make a Good Pet? | Pets Rated 😲 #capuchinmonkey #animals
video_description: Smart, curious, and incredibly social—capuchin monkeys can be adorable and challenging at the same time. Could they make a ...
text_content:
Yep. You probably need to know the difference between a Capuchin and Macaque monkeys first. Then try and speak from a place of authority and expertise. Not knowing the simple difference in two breeds of monkeys probably hurts the message that I believe you are trying to get across. Monkeys are an amazing little life form. Their eyes, faces, little human like hands, and curious playful nature are hard to resist. Their similarity to humans is amazing and why so many people enjoy watching them. The fact we find them so adorable and want to hug and take care of all of them is something programmed deep within our being. Since similar to humans, both male and female humans feel the way they do about them because babies propagate and ensure the survival of a species. The will of the cells to multiply and copy themselves drives all living things. However, that cannot occur if the babies don't live and survive long enough to repeat the once again. So, monkey babies being similar in appearance to that of human babies, subconsciously drives us to protect, provide, and comfort them. The owning of a primate, great or small, as a pet isn't the best for the monkeys. These little beings were designed by the creator to florish in their natural habitats. Despite the fact humans are encroaching into the monkeys home, more on that in a bit, they still belong in their home. As someone who rescues and rehabilitates primates whenever that very thing occurs, most of our rescues don't come from a situation of the little guys being injuried from saws or bulldozers. Instead they came from uniformed and in most cases good intentioned people trying to make them their pet.
 Typically, they will have everything they think a monkey needs. A very large enclosure, the right foods, toys, treats, and lots of love. However, even that is not enough to make sure these wonderful beings have the opportunity to live long fulfilling monkey lives. With baby monkeys, depending on their age and breed, have very strenuous feeding cycles. A capuchin monkey around 2 weeks old needs to be fed every 2 to 3 hours, day and night. At this age, they are usually fed a specialized milk replacer or formula via a small 1cc syringe to prevent aspiration. Thats day and night. Most people struggle with that sort of a feeding cycle with a human baby let alone a fury one. This must be followed up with many monkey babies by having to rub their genital area with a warm, damp tissue after every feeding to stimulate urination and defecation. Wow, not something people are accustomed too. Then the baby capuchin must also be kept in an incubator or at a consistent warm temperature (85°F or above) as they cannot regulate their own body temperature at this age. Just to name a few. 

 Whenever people get frustrated, busy, have a human baby, lose a job, or suffer one of a litany of possible lifestyle changes such as a drastic income change or they can no longer dedicate the amount of time needed to give monkeys the attention or care they need for a healthy life. The other big one we run into is that people aren't prepared for is when the cute babies turn into older monkeys. Sometimes a monkey can get aggressive at varying times in their lives including hormone changes and that desire to mate. This isn't the monkeys fault because this is how they are designed deep in their DNA. That isn't something you should punish as this is completely natural. They have similar emotions and feelings just like we do and can get mad, depressed, stressed out and can even get very possessive with their owner. Whenever they get jealous or possessive those little human like arms, hands, and fingers become poop flinging, fist-pounding, object throwing expressions of those feelings. Thats when they either get mistreated, abandoned, and neglected. 

Regardless of the reason their owners no longer can keep them and places like the one a work at come into play. Although their owners may have started out with good intentions, spent untold fortunes on an enclosure as big as some people's entire house, provided vet care, love them, and will end up missing them just like anyone would whenever they say goodbye to a family member. The results are the same. The monkey suffers and doesn't get the opportunity to live like they would if they were left alone in their natural habitat. Thats when organizations and sanctuaries get involved. Sadly, there are far too many stories I could share about what our little fury buddies must have went through. Some of them had been living in the most disgusting and vile environments you could imagine. The most heartbreaking thing of all wasn't just the abhorrent state of their physical health but their mental state as well. Abandon, mistreated, and in the care of total strangers these monkeys have had their hearts crushed. All they know is how to be a monkey and a few party tricks they have been taught to put on shows for money. They don't fully understand the minds of mankind. Many times their owners have had them since they were babies. All they know is you! Now, for at least the second time in their life, that pillar of stability, security, and  consistency is gone. They again have lost both things suddenly and they think it was them that caused this. Please, use this data to make an informed decision.

video_description: Ever dreamed of owning a monkey? Meet the Capuchin – the energetic, adorable, and super smart primate that could change ...
text_content:
A monkey of any description is a very bad choice as a pet. They are a 30-40 year commitment that requires a lot of attention and special treatment. They urinate and defecate wherever and whenever they feel the urge. Mature females menstruate, and mature males masturbate. They will throw feces if they become angry. They stink and are very messy. They require special veterinary care and a very specific diet. They require a lot of mental stimulation that places a huge demand on the owner. When they are babies, they are sweet, docile and adorable. But when they mature, they can become aggressive and even dangerous. 

Monkeys are social animals that belong in troops. They must have this social interaction in order to be mentally healthy. They are not "live toys" that owners force to walk upright, wear doll clothes, and perform tricks. Monkeys need to be left in their natural habitat and not be kept prisoner by creatures (humans) with which they ultimately are not compatible.

text_content:
For anyone wanting a pet monkey I’d advise you to first get a Fish or a spider if you can care for that move on to a Dog or a Cat don’t buy one their are plenty that need loving families and can be adopted if that goes well and you meet the other Conditions including having plenty of space in your home then you can consider getting a Monkey however I still disagree with owning them as pets because even if you manage to provide the animals basic needs it’s not going to be as happy or live as long as a Wild monkey.

Just look at Chimpanzees or other Apes in Zoos and how despite all of the care these animals are provided they simply can’t be given enough space and desire to escape that’s why I maintain my belief that many animal species belong in the Wild.

text_content: Finger monkeys have a foul odor. It’s musky no matter how much you bathe them, It’s still there. Spider monkeys grow up to be the size of a 5 to 6 year old child they need to have the room to be able to swing handover hand for great lengths in order to be happy so unless you plan on building a cage that’s at least 40’ x 40’, with multiple monkeys cause they are not happy alone,  don’t get a spider monkey. Capuchin monkeys are not easily trained. They may be smart, but they have to decide if they want to listen to you and they usually don’t. No matter how you raise them,They will always end up biting. if you want them indoors you’re changing diapers for up to 50 years; That’s if you live long enough. A capuchin can live more than 50 years. These monkeys do not do well being alone without a human or other monkeys, so plan on spending the rest of your life tied down to a monkey.

text_content:
I love monkeys. Especially capuchins. We had one while I was growing up. And there was someone who had another one who live a short way away.

They are very clever, and if they are treated with love, the proper diet, and enough space for them, they are delightful. They live a long time in captivity. Just remember that it IS captivity. They do not belong there. They belong in the wild, sanctuary or zoo. BTW, zoo's of today are NOT like the ones in previous decades. The more that is learned about primates, the more their surroundings are made to fit them.

video_title: The monkey did not see this twist coming 😲
text_content:
I genuinely had a moment like that. 

It was my nephew’s birthday. It was Mario themed party at a place like Chuck E. Cheese. My niece and nephew love to hang around me so normally they would stay close to me and hold my hand and what not especially in public places. while I’m talking to a friend of mines, I felt a hand grab and hold mines. Me being simple and not bothering to look continued talking thinking it was just my niece or nephew. At some point, my friend asked, “Wheres the birthday boy?” I turned my head left and seen that my niece was on the left side of the room, eating pizza and my nephew was standing next to her, holding a Mario balloon in one hand and a Luigi in the other. It was then I finally started questioning. “If the kids are over there, then who is holding my hand..?”

I finally looked down to the right and realized some random child was holding my hand smiling at me the whole time. Being awkwarded out, I let go of his hand, told him excuse me I have to use the loo and told friend to come with me and ran out. 

I had my friend come outside with me and started yelling at him. “Why didn’t you tell me some random kid was holding my hand.?” 😤

He says, “I thought that was your family.”  🤷🏽‍♂️

text_content: Cute, isn't he?? Now imagine having a two yr old for 30-40yrs?? I help out at a primate sanctuary, all of our primates are from labs, or used to be pets, people dnt knw what they are getting into when buying pet monkeys..

video_title: This baby monkey likes to carry a backpack 😂 @MonkeyPika2610
text_content:
My very 1st thought was "Oh, isn't Pica cute".....that is, until I looked in the comments. I didn't realize monkeys are "taught" to walk that way by tying their hands back & probably putting weight in the backpack to prevent them from waking naturally on all fours.

Thank you for those posts, you have enlightened me. Idk, I guess I just hadn't thought of the actual situation in which Pica is likely living. I have learned something from you all & it has changed my opinion. No, this isn't "cute", it's actually quite sad. To think what this creature has been thru/endured already in his young life makes my heart ache. Poor thing.

Pica should be with his mother & own kind. He's a wild animal - not a pet & definitely not a tiny human. No way he'd be walking on 2 legs without being coerced to do so - it simply wouldn't happen otherwise.

video_title: This baby monkey likes to carry a backpack 😂 @MonkeyPika2610
text_content: I have always loved and wanted to own a monkey like this until now… I didn’t know what little Pika really has to endure… Though he is a very cute little monkey he deserves to be a cute little monkey in the wild.😢

video_title: This baby monkey likes to carry a backpack 😂 @MonkeyPika2610
text_content: When I was a kid my uncle had a baby monkey that was abandoned at a doc in Hawaii. He raised it before a sanctuary was able to take it which turned out to be 6 years later. It was a Barbary Macaque. As a baby it carried a purse. She actually put things in it. Later it would take food from our plates and keep them in the purse. So many cute antics. From this experience I could see why they should not be pets. It is cruel.

text_content: So I am the Jason Coats from this story. I'd like to say that this is a pretty fair recollection  of the events and what these psychotic people did to me not to mention what legal system did to me. I'd like to clear up one thing that has always been told wrong in the media. I did not shoot the chimp in the back. The weapon I had was a remington wing master 870 pump 20 gauge. This was a legal duck hunting gun that could only hold three rounds. The first shot in the chamber was buckshot (in case i didn't have time to aim)  the second two rounds were slugs (a single projectile) made for deer hunting which is not necessarily the most accurate  coming out of a duck gun. My border collie Lucky was still running circles around the chimp when I called for him she stopped circling and looked at me and turned my way. I shot the first shot being buck shot (for those who don't know imagine maybe 100 bbs at once) and as fast as I  could shoot were the slugs. According to the autopsy report those "bbs" hit her entire left front side, the second shot which  actually thought had missed with, hit her in the face and the third as she was in her lower left  hip and she fell to the ground. No shots to the back as they say. Again it simply isn't true.

The teacher was trying to stop the show,.but was fumbling with the remote, I was trying to distract kids, to no avail. It is imprinted on my brain as an adult. The last shot of the doc I remember was a big male chimp holding and chewing on a babies rib cage... NOT PETS, NOT STAND IN HUMAN BABIES, these are wild animals. Leave them be in their world.

text_content: Hi, you guys probably recognize my username, don't freak out, I'm not THAT Jason Coats, I'm his youngest son, I just wanted to say thank you to all the people showing support for my dad, especially everyone making the 90% murder= 90% trafficking (my dad said nearly the same thing in court). This man got nearly the whole story, used a lot of quotes from the Cassie's trying to slander my dad as an animal abuser/drug addict, all of which I can say without a doubt is false. My dad is one of the biggest animal fan I know and can say that in all 21 years of my life, 2 years after the chimp incident, the closest my dads come to drugs is watching breaking bad. That being said I absolutely stand with the fact that my dad did what was absolutely necessary and the only thing I wish was different was things animals were never born or raised in captivity, wild animals need to wild or as wild as they can be without fear for human or chimp safety. I'm glad they all finally got the care they needed and taken from those absolutely awful people. Anyone who still disagrees I urge you watch the Chimp Crazy documentary on Max for all the evidence that chimps should not mix with people.

text_content: I can believe Jason got arrested. A person who lived two houses down from me had a wolf dog hybrid. He had frequently broken state laws regarding enclosures for wolf dogs. The dog almost attacked me once. It was killing small animals including a neighbor's cat. Then one night another neighbors sister and brother-in-law were leaving their house, and somehow their car keys had fallen out of a jacket. The sister-in-law was holding their baby by the car door waiting for her husband to find the keys when they heard a growl. The wolf dog had her cornered and she couldn't get in the car. My neighbor fired two shots in the air, then one close to the wolf dog, but it never backed away so finally he felt he had no choice but to shoot it. He was charged, but he reached a settlement with the owner of the wolf dog and courts where he had to pay for medical bills, and court costs.  He was facing a felony, but he was a bailiff and if he didn't take the deal, he would have most likely lost his job.  This was even though it was found out the dogs owner had left the dog there for a week while he went to visit his girlfriend, but just left a big pile of food and a bucket of water in the enclosure. The dog probably ate all the food within the first couple of days and was starving. Nobody blamed the dog, including the neighbor who shot him. We all blamed the dogs owner plus whoever was in charge of fines or assessing those fines to the owner when he had repeatedly broken the law.  That's the biggest issue in my opinion. The laws and enforcement of laws regarding all types of animals, not just wild animals, are too lacks.  And usually it is an innocent victim and the animal who pays the price, and rarely the person who owns the animal.

text_content: When I was six years old, my dad did security work for a circus that came thru our city. During one of the events a loud scream filled the areana, followed by two loud popping sounds. I knew my dad carried a service weapon and I started crying. My mom, along with us kids, were escorted to the tunnel where my dad stood with blood on his uniform. He assured us that it wasn't his. Apparently, the trainer went in to get the chimp ready to go on, when it suddenly went berserk and attacked the trainer. My dad, arrived, assessed the moment, the planted two bullets in the chimp's brain. My dad saved the trainer and I learned that chimps are not cute and cuddly toys. 48 years later I still feel the same way.

text_content: In the UK, from what I remember from zoo management classes, is that a chimp is classified as a class 1 dangerous animal. That means staff in a zoo or collection still have a chance of recapturing the animal while on the premises as long as its not causing a threat to human life, but once it escapes from the zoo entirely, the chances are it will just be killed. Further recapture may be tried if the animal has not gotten near residential area, but the moment it gets too close to residential areas, the risk of harm outweighs the life of the animal. Like in this case, tranquillisers are not instant, and are made less effective by adrenaline. Jason had every right.

text_content:
My grandmothers dad had an animal shop in the 60’s. They had a lot of different animals and even sold lions for a while. Back then that was quite normal. He would buy birds that are used for some type of hunting sport where they release them and someone else shoots them from a distance idk what it’s called. He thought it was wrong so he bought them and released them. He never made a lot of money he loved animals quite a lot. 

Then they got a chimpanzee that never got sold and my grandmother who was 14 years old absolutely loved him. He got a lot of care from her and they had a really strong bond. Around ten years later it became illegal to keep chimpanzees in Belgium so he had to go to a zoo. There he got introduced to other chimpanzees and even became a father. My grandma went to visit him and when he saw her he immediately ran to get his baby and showed it to her. He was super proud to show his baby to her and he always kept recognising her when she visited him. She’s now an old lady who loves painting animals. She even does exhibitions with her art friends. All she draws, paints and prints are animals and mostly cats.😅😂

text_content:
I worked at a petting zoo that had some lemurs. They were *mean*. I was bit the one and only time I went into an enclosure to clean it. It was a small nip, basically equivalent to a cat scratch, but it wasn't casually brushed off, as I stopped working for the day and got it bandaged up.

I wasn't allowed to get close to the area where they had 3 aggressive lemurs (I cleaned an enclosure with only one, much calmer overall lemur), and all the enclosures had basically two doors on them, with a little area in between, and you always kept at least one door closed at all times.

They also had much more going on in their areas, with toys and whatnot, and they were routinely rotated throughout 3 different enclosures so they had more variety in their life. All this was for lemurs, who are both much smaller add far less dangerous than chimpanzees, but were very clearly treated much better and held to a higher safety standard by this small business rentable petting zoo than anyone in this video working with large, dangerous, understimulated great apes.

video_title: Raising A Chimp & Baby As Siblings
text_content:
You’re thinking of Donald Kellogg, the human baby raised alongside a chimpanzee (Gua) in the 1931–1932 Kellogg experiment.

Short answer:
Yes. Donald Kellogg died by suicide in 1973.

What we know (carefully and accurately):
	•	Donald was raised for about 9 months alongside the chimp by his father, Winthrop Kellogg, a psychologist.
	•	The experiment was ended early because Donald began imitating the chimp’s behaviors (vocalizations, gestures), raising serious developmental concerns.
	•	Donald grew up afterward in normal human settings and did not continue the experiment or live like a chimp.
	•	As an adult, he reportedly struggled with long-term depression.
	•	In 1973, at age 43, Donald died by suicide (gunshot).

Important context (often misunderstood online):
	•	There is no solid evidence that the experiment alone “caused” his death.
	•	However, many historians and psychologists believe the study may have contributed to psychological stress, identity issues, or emotional difficulty later in life.
	•	The case is now widely cited as a reason such cross-species upbringing experiments are unethical and no longer permitted

text_content:
The story Joe is referring to is St. James Davis & his wife LaDonna Davis and their chimpanzee Moe.

After Moe had bit several people in West Covina, California, the chimp was relocated to an animal sanctuary. 

When the two came to visit, they brought birthday cake, toys, chocolate, and even candy hearts. They were seated at a picnic table celebrating his birthday when two adult male chimps, Buddy & Ollie rushed the wife and bit her thumb off. St. James pushed his wife underneath the table to protect her and the chimps began attacking him simultaneously. They attacked his face including his nose and lips, his left foot, destroyed most of his fingers, ripped off his testicles, his buttocks, part of his torso. The sanctuary’s owners son-in-law retrieved a .45 pistol and shot the chimp Buddy in the head. Ollie dragged St. James body down an empty corridor where the son-in-law then killed him by gunshot. Their chimp Moe was not involved in the attack, but it’s unclear if he tried to save or protect either of them. 

St. James spent six months in the hospital recovering, some of which he was in a coma. He underwent 60 surgeries, has a glass eye, and two slits where his nose used to be.

Interestingly enough, in 2008 the Davises chimp Moe escaped from his sanctuary. There have been reports of sightings near homes where he was last housed, as well as claims he was heading toward a mountain. The Davises hired a helicopter to do flyovers of the San Bernardino National Forest in an attempt to find him… but to date the 42-year old chimp has never been found. 

Thank you Wikipedia.

Here in Australia we have laws that prohibit anyone from having any exotic wild life as pets, and I’m glad we do. 
These awesome animals need to be in their own wild habitat, not in someone’s back yard or house. I have no sympathy for anyone who’s attacked by these wild animals.

Experiments in Full body transplants first can be dated back to Alexis Carrel who grafted a dog’s head onto another dog. text_content: Orangutans have a level of intellect that is absolutely incredible. Orangutans will intently watch everything a person or another Orangutan will do. video_title: Wild Chimp adopts Pet Kitten
video_description: A young chimpanzee shows empathy towards a baby genet kitten and protects it from other chimpanzees Clip from episode 1 ...
text_content: According to the film crew, he had been playing and protecting the kitten for a couple days, but on the last day it vanished and they don't know what happened to it. It's possible it was killed by the other chimps or that it starved. text_content: I noticed that a lot of people thought that when Travis was smiling he was doing it to be cute, spoiler alert; he wasn’t. Chimps smile as a threat or a warning, they’re not human so they don’t behave like us nor have the same emotional/social reactions or norms with us. Travis was a danger from day 1, not because he was violent but because he was a 200 pound animal with amazing strength and intelligence who was also probably sexually frustrated. He was also being medicated with unprescribed human medicine. He was also never (I don’t know if that’s true but I’m assuming) given the opportunity to socialize with his own species and mate any females to relive sexual frustrations, all in all, Travis did not deserve to die, he shouldn’t have EVER been a ‘pet’, medicated to hell and isolated from his own kind. PLUS the woman who owned him let him consume alcohol 🤦

But for this five seconds we could just...😊

text_content:
In person, I've seen gibbons brachiating a few times, and was always totally jealous! I've always loved climbing trees, but am old now, and can hopefully live/swing vicariously through watching them! Lovely furry people and, sadly, been done a terrible wrong by the dominant apes of the planet! 
As an old person, I'm sometimes ashamed of the people we used to be- I remember going into a pet store and there were small monkeys in metal cages, screaming and climbing about. My brother and I were children, and wanted a "pet monkey," but my Dad said no.  
He was much smarter than we were, and had much more empathy! 
Oh those poor little creatures!

text_content:
Gibbons are genuinely one of my favourite animals. I have this tradition where with every trip I go on, I bring a little wooden carved caribou and take pictures with him. My family and I were at the zoo and at the gibbon enclosure and I was setting up the little caribou for a few pictures when a gibbon came right over to the glass and just stared at it. She was so cute and she seemed so fascinated with it. I sat there with her for a few minutes and showed the caribou to her, she even tried to grab it through the glass.

Anyways eventually a bunch of little kids started crowding around me so I had to move, but I absolutely love gibbons and they are very underrated.

“Terminally trolling”

11/10 video.   (I’m very biased. Gibbons are rad as hell)

video_title: Man Lives With His Two Pet Gibbons
video_description: ID: 3447750 ONSCREEN CREDIT - @eldar.gadjiev This man lives with two pet gibbons in his home in Moscow, Russia. Designer ...
text_content:
There is a gibbon sanctuary not far from my house. Even though gibbons are beyond adorable, fascinating, and even though humans and gibbons are both primates, people CANNOT be replacement gibbons. Gibbons thrive in their natural habitat, NOT as expensive novelty pets. Gibbons kept in captivity often develop behaviors that their owners can't handle when they grow up. Pet baby gibbons are frequently separated from their mothers before they are ready to be independent just for someone's amusement. 
The owner of the gibbon sanctuary by my house told me that you can tell which gibbons were separated from their mothers as infants because they will suck their thumbs. The gibbons at this sanctuary are descendants of generations of captive pet gibbons. The sanctuary is a full time, intense job and labor of love for the lady who keeps the sanctuary. People have absolutely no idea how labor intensive these adorable tiny gibbons will grow up to be. Even the most attuned human will absolutely NOT be a gibbon mother who can teach a gibbon how to be a gibbon. This baby gibbon's vocalizations were tragic, because this baby gibbon is instinctually calling out for other gibbons to socialize with, especially its mother. 
As much as I adore gibbons, keeping this tiny baby gibbon as an amusing pet is beyond narcissistic and short sighted. Gibbons in a house will always need a diaper, because I have never seen or heard if one who could be potty trained.

video_title: Capuchin monkeys kidnap howler monkey babies
video_description: Scientists spotted surprising evidence of what they describe as monkey kidnappings while reviewing video footage. For more ...
text_content: Capuchins are very intelligent, adapt to their environments fast, social, affectionate, humble and open minded. They may think the howler infants were abandoned for some reason and needed to be cared for or even adopted? Or they simply want to show the infants certain things because they're "bored" or as an extension of their social circles. The Capuchin obviously have no bad intentions, they're not hurting or mistreating the Howlers, they're feeding and protecting them as their own as much as possible though because some are Males the Howler infants end up lacking Milk. The Howler parents are bigger and stronger than the Capuchin, why are they nowhere to be found and back to retrieve their babies? Obviously, the Capuchin have caught onto something progressively here.

video_title: Top 5 Monkeys to OWN as Pets!
video_description: Here are the top 5 monkeys to own as pets! ENJOY! #capuchinmonkey #spidermonkey #monkeypet #chimpanzee ...
text_content:
I wish I could have a couple marmosets or tamarins as pets. 
They do need a variety of food and you have to cut up fruit and veggies for them and give them animal protein, such as meal worms and insects. They eat small mammals, lizards, frogs, and bird eggs. 
This is something that you don't have to do for your cat or dog.
They also eat monkey biscuits in captivity.
They need to live in pairs if not in groups and need to be constantly entertained. They need you to stay home with them most of the day.

text_content:
Your gentleness and kindness pours out of the screen. This must be so very hard for you all. But I really hope you know what a very special job you do. You should all be so very proud of yourselves. You are incredible, trying so hard to care for these animals and beings in the way that you do. I wish I could be of some assistance.

My very best wishes to you. 

With love and blessings. 

Teresa. (UK). xx

video_title: Owning a Pet Lemur Myths | True or False!
video_description: Myths and claims about pet lemurs, do they make bad pets? Do they become aggressive to their owners? Are they illegal?
text_content:
I have been reading about lemurs for months now but not out of a will to own them. They are endangered animals and often critically so. They also are not like some house cat that you can easily keep. Lemurs have their own particular way of life which does not include humans. They belong into the wild or at least sanctuaries where professional conservators can care and support them in their way of life. The Duke Lemur Center would be a good example for exactly such an effort. And unsurpringly, they also speak out against owning one as a pet just as anyone who actually works with lemurs in conservation, studies, field work or something else in academia.
I really love lemurs and this video just invokes so much disgust in me.

video_title: Can Pygmy Marmosets be Pets? 🐒 Do Finger Monkeys Make Good Pets? 🙉
video_description: Can Pygmy Marmosets be Pets? Do Finger Monkeys Make Good Pets? As one can imagine, it's easy to fall for their cute faces ...
text_content: Decades ago I had a couple of these I raised from babies. They smelled bad, and would pee out of their cage, would rip their diapers off when outside the cage, flew around the house, climbed my curtains, my legs, and a lot of other things. The would shimmy up my legs and scratch the heck out of my legs and neck with their really sharp claws. They would bite on occasion. The female was more chill than the male. He bit my friend in the ear once just for opening the cage to feed them. Mind you these were very socialized monkeys that I raised from babies with love. Although they can be friendly, there’s nothing you can do to make them domesticated, they will always be wild animals and bite. These are outdoor pets if anything at all. They’re very cute and entertaining but to be honest they’re better off left in the wild.

video_description: Can Pygmy Marmosets be Pets? Do Finger Monkeys Make Good Pets? As one can imagine, it's easy to fall for their cute faces ...
text_content:
No.  Monkeys - in general - do NOT make "good" pets.

Unless you are very well-prepared and aware of what monkeys are and do, don't get a monkey. They are too intelligent, curious, and possessive to be a good room mate. They will shit and piss anywhere, and at any time.  You cannot physically discipline a monkey, nor is it easy to do so by restricting them from activity or out of cage freedoms. They will bear a grudge. They will bite if you attempt to lift it off the couch, and throw their shit at you as you pass the cage.

This is why they end up mean and vicious, and needing rescue.

DON'T get a monkey.

text_content: My dad casually said he had a pet monkey once a long time ago. Then he said, "NEVER AGAIN." And didn't really speak about it again. He was stationed in the tropics at the time, maybe Thailand? I think when he was given orders to leave he gave the monkey to a buddy of his. That was how he acquired the monkey. You really have to know what you are getting yourself into and be prepared to care for this little "human" for the rest of your life. They have hands, emotions, dietary restrictions, restrictions in the states, they want to be with you 24/7, need diapers, can't be baraccaded while you go to work or have a social life. They are like human babies. Extremely needy and they get into everything. Cute as a button, but really think about it for a long time and do research. A lot of research. Are you willing to give up your life for this little one because that is the sacrifice, including where you will live and your monetary budget. ❤❤

text_content: Time out/disengagement has always worked for me with biting. You bite, you’re done. I say, “No bite, time out,” and separate from them. A transport carrier works well as a time out room, just don’t use their sleeping area for discipline. They usually figure it out eventually without harsh punishment, and at least you’re not being chewed on while they’re in time out.

video_title: LIVING WITH A MARMOSET | PET MARMOSET MONKEY PT. 2
video_description: Update on my life featuring jack the marmoset monkey My ig: @shahahazain Jacks ig: @thatboijackk.
text_content:
You have to find out how a mother pygmy marmoset disciplines it's baby  and that's is how you discipline this one.  Also, you HAVE to make him wear diapers, because it's a health hazzard.  Don't keep him spoiled, discipline marmoset style, it will work.  Don't give in, and don't let him eat food he is not supposed to have, period.  You are the leader, not the other way around. He will eventually 
 calm down but will need much personal socialization and consistency.  2 hours a day is not enough, unless you want a maladjusted little monster.   It's never too late to break bad habits.  Just be consistent.

text_content:
Their biggest threat is the pet trade but this video almost sounds like a commercial for getting one as a pet. 

Their second threat as is every animals is habitat loss. I think there should be a global law that requires that all use on any land that is now in use can only be expanded up or downwards and that humans should not be allowed to take resource or use any land that requires areas outside the perimeter we use. So if we are cutting down trees, it would have to stop exactly where the last tree fell. If we need more trees than we need to convert some other land that is already in use by humans. I also think it would be a nice addition to my made up global law that we need to relinquish a certain amount of land area that we are already using back permanently back to nature each year. The relinquished land back to nature must be in great condition and all traces of human contamination should have be cleaned up and removed. 

I always want everything for a pet but I would never ever do that. However, despite being so little and cute, I wouldn’t want one or a troop of these. I don’t like the way they move their heads so fast, stops before I would expect it to, and they constantly move their head and in so many different directions. They could just have moved their eyes instead. It makes them like shifty. Plus I was a little grossed out by the way they eat tree sap. No need for stretching the sap back and forth, thin to thick. Their twitchy little critters.

text_content: $3000 isn't too bad considering they did an involved surgery. Because he is small made it cheaper in some ways, when it comes to the supplies and hardware that is a big part of the cost. The sugical site is also small and wouldn't have been a huge effort on the surgon. The anesthesiologist whold have to be carful as you said due to the small size, the margin of error for calculating the anesthetics really small while at the same time you want to make sure that it is enough for him to be fully under. Vets in my area charge a lot more but there is enough of them to where you can always get a second opinion, even with exotics. I hope your little guy feels much better, your keen observstion saved him from trying to hide the injury and suffering longer. These are the costs that breeders and pet advocates try to make clear when someone wants to get a pet. It can be hard to pay for something like that but when you love your animal friend it is just something that has to be done.

text_content: I grew up on a South African farm, we had a few Meerkat families living on the farm, the home family was only about 20 meters from the back door, They would come right up to the back door and helped themselves to the dog bicuits we left out for the Boerboel and BullMastiff dogs would always be on the back of my dad's pickup truck just for incase my dad was going to jump in and leave them behind. The baby Meerkats (very small, they kind of look like tiny rats or mice) would come into the kitchen and take up residence behind the old wood stove we had in the back kitchen. The mothers would come back after a day of foraging or a battle/war they had on with other colonies/families of meerkats who all wanted the best spot, close to the back door for food and milk. So they became very tamed and  ruled over all the Cats and Dogs (They are a lot smarter than Dogs or Kats) What made them the Generals of the Household animal army was that they were in a cahoots with the other smarter "thing" Crows. That was a combination that no Dog, Kat or even my younger brother could outsmart. The Crow would keep a lookout while the Meerkats ransacked the Kitchen, and because they always wanted to carry off more than they could carry, they left a breadcrumb trail for the Crow to vacuum up.

video_title: Common Marmoset Monkey Facts from a Vet
video_description: Marmosets do not make good pets, but they do need vets!
text_content: Pygmy marmosets are generally not good pets; they are difficult, high-maintenance wild animals with complex needs, requiring specialized care, specific social groups (not just humans), a warm tropical climate, and a long-term commitment (15-20 years), often leading to stress, aggression, and potential destruction in a home setting, despite their cute appearance. They are not domesticated and struggle with human environments, often getting bored, marking territory (smelly), and needing constant enrichment and interaction with other marmosets, not just people.

I have once met an elderly indigenous couple at the market hall in Cusco/Peru who had one they found on the ground in the forest, couldn't find its mommy and kept the baby to care for it.
They carried the little one with them in a pocket wherever they went, like being its parents.
It was so cute and those people were so kind that I couldn't hold back a couple of tears.
The woman then touched my hand  with a consolating gesture which touched my feelings even more.
I will never forget that moment of profound human kindness and utter cuteness.
I felt being blessed, like being touched by humanity at its best and by nature itself.

text_content: One of my brothers has a marmoset. Saying they are high maintenance is an understatement. He cannot go on holiday because his " daughter" doesn't like anywhere else but home. He can't have a girlfriend because his " daughter" is very aggressive towards other females. She hates me and our mother too. She is extremely clingy to my brother. He made irrevocable life changes to accommodate that cute little psychopath daughter of his. He did get two babies but the sister died and after my brother went through the process of raising marmosets he doesn't think he will ever get another one. They take them from their mom when they are still very small so the marmoset can bond with you. It's the only way to get them to bond with a human. So think carefully before getting one. They are a long and life altering commitment and since you are ripping the little thing from their mother you better take care of that baby like your life depends on it.

text_content: I have reported multiple monkey abuse videos & only a few were removed, We have to go back further than just the moment these baby monkeys are even brought into the homes with humans, these babies had a monkey mother at the time they were born, So at some point, they were stolen from their moms or the mom was killed and they were taken, Then they are brought home by humans and forced to live as a baby, diapered, clothed,  fed formula out of baby bottles, forced to stand & walk on two legs, which is detrimental to their backs, Ever notice in the videos when they are walking on two feet how they will hold their backs and try to sit down? That’s because it hurts their backs when they are forced to do this, People often mistake what looks like a smile as a smile, but it’s actually called, the fear of grimace 😬, It’s because their scared of their owners, they have been abused and beaten by humans, I have to admit I have seen some videos or humans who treated their monkeys quite well, the monkeys are happy and well cared for, but for most of these monkeys on YouTube, they are not in good situations and need to be removed

text_content: I suspect a lot of the staged monkey video channels  from Vietnam to be a showcase for the exotic pet black market.  The "caretakers" on these channels seem to be related to each other, and i suspect with a larger organization.  They make it look like it's easy and fun to have a monkey as a pet, and that increases the demand.  The monkeys generally disappear before the age of three, often without any explanation.  For many of them, they are abandoned in the forest when they get less cute or too difficult to manage, but i think the best trained monkeys are certainly sold on the black market.  These monkeys already have lots of admirers who want to adopt them, and these criminals would'nt miss the opportunity to make a last very big amount of money with them.

text_content: All macaques may look vaguely like humans, may act in ways similar to humans, but they are not humans. Each troop of monkeys acts as dictated by the alpha male. Each troop has a caste system, those at the top, and those at the bottom. Depending on where each one fits in the hierarchy will determine their privileges and how they act. Each mother also has her own idea on how to raise her offspring. For this infant, the weaning process may have already begun. The mother controls how much time is spent at the milkbar. She isn't being evil, she is acting as instinct and observation dictate.

Notva tantrum, they evolved tok do this,  To the point where they even do it against their own will - say trying to escspe from kidnapper, but as soon as all 4 limbs are free, every nerve in its body tells it Cling! Cling or die!it flings itself back onto its kidnapper clutching her lol
    Its a biological inborn response to not clinging to something, it clings on mom 24/7 first few mos never without contact. At about 4 mos old it stops being the bodies automatic response. See the mom is quadreped so the baby lives on her body clinging with powerful grip as she climbs jumps etc. But if the baby were to fall and the mom or troop didn't notice, it would die. So the ones who were terrified enough that if they fell, they freaked out with loud noise abd scurrying "popcorning" movement  drew the moms or troops attention immediately, and go back for it  and  it will go on to pass on this trait to many babies.  Whereas those who were chill just got left behind to be eaten by predator or die of exposure and never passed on those traits.

video_description: UPDATE: http://www.youtube.com/DmitrySergeyev - the owner of this video. Please, visit his channel. Her name is Sonya and she ...
text_content:
Although the actions depicted in this video are viewed as harmless, animal experts assert that these animals experience high levels of stress from being tortured during the course of such "performances": Dr. Anna Nekaris, an anthropologist who specializes in slow-loris research at Oxford Brookes University states that the loris willingness to be held is not because they are comfortable but rather it is part of their defense mechanism — to be still and silent when they feel threatened. The only reason the loris isn't biting the person holding it in the video is because it has had its teeth cruelly ripped out by illegal traders who poach them from their natural rain forest habitat. The teeth are removed because the loris can deliver a toxic bite. The reason the tickled slow loris is seen raising its arms (a sign of distress) is because it is making an attempt to defend itself by reaching its toxic gland: The slow loris is the world's only poisonous primate. Its venom is stored in an elbow patch. When it is feeling threatened, the slow loris will raise its arms above its head in a diamond shape, suck in the poison from its elbow patch, then mix it around in its mouth before delivering the toxic bite. These animals are also often in pain because they are nocturnal and not equipped to deal with extended periods of daylight. In addition to causing a high level of stress in the animal, this video also encourages the illegal trade of slow lorises: The popularity of slow loris videos has led to an increase in the number of people wanting to have a slow loris as a pet. And while it may appear cute, the animal in the video is suffering and so is the entire species. Slow lorises are shoved into plastic 
crates and bags; their teeth are clipped out with pliers or wire cutters with no anesthesia, no aftercare, no medicine and most die; multiple animals are crammed into tiny cages where they wound each other with their venomous bites. Large percentages of captured slow lorises die due to inhumane, overcrowded, stressful conditions during transportation often with the bodies of others that have died on the journey. This painful process of mutilation not only often kills the animals via blood loss or infection, but also prevents rescued lorises from being released back into the wild. Not only does the slow loris pet trade cause unimaginable suffering, it is also the biggest threat to the survival of the species, which is in serious danger of extinction. All eight species of slow lorises are considered “threatened” and the Javan slow loris is one of the 25 most endangered primates worldwide. So if you have a heart, please do not like or share this video.

text_content: Because of their "cuteness", videos of pet slow lorises are some of the mostly frequently watched animal-related viral videos on YouTube.[60][123] In March 2011, a newly posted video of a slow loris holding a cocktail umbrella had been viewed more than two million times, while an older video of a slow loris being tickled had been viewed more than six million times.[131] According to Nekaris, these videos are misunderstood by most people who watch them, since most do not realize that it is illegal in most countries to own them as pets and that the slow lorises in the videos are only docile because that is their passive defensive reaction to threatening situations.[123][131] Despite frequent advertisements by pet shops in Japan, the World Conservation Monitoring Centre reported only a few dozen slow lorises were imported in 2006, suggesting frequent smuggling.[83] Slow lorises are also smuggled to China, Taiwan, Europe, Russia, the United States, and Saudi Arabia for use as pets.[130][120][131]

text_content:
Slow loris
"Their huge round eyes, adorable faces and almost human-like paws have made slow lorises one of the internet's favourite animals. Being nocturnal, the little primates seem almost docile during the day: when they do move around, they do so slowly and deliberately. When threatened, they tend to freeze.
But slow lorises also have a lethal ace up their sleeve, almost literally.
In extreme cases, bite recipients may enter anaphylactic shock, sometimes resulting in death
When threatened, a loris will often raise its arms above its head. Far from being a sign of surrender, this is a defensive posture, bringing a gland around its elbow close to its mouth.
By combining the oil secreted from the gland with saliva, the loris creates a strong venom, which it then smears onto the top of its head, making it a toxic morsel for any predator.
The loris can also keep the venom inside its mouth, delivering it in a bite. The incisors at the front of the bottom jaw help to conduct the liquid upwards, ensuring it gets into any wound it can inflict.
According to Anne-Isola Nekaris of Oxford Brookes University, a bite from a slow loris is "intensely painful". "In extreme cases, bite recipients may enter anaphylactic shock, sometimes resulting in death," Nekaris says.
There are nine known species of slow loris living in the forests of South East Asia. The venom of the pygmy slow loris (Nycticebus pygmaeus) has been found to contain 220 compounds.
So, the next time you watch a video of a slow loris being "tickled" with its arms in the air, remember it is not enjoying the experience: it is waiting for a chance to strike."

text_content:
People were literally clipping their teeth (with pliers) so they can't puncture skin, to keep as pets. And people would "tickle" them in a certain way, that was actually equivalent to torture because they're terrified and in a defensive posture trying to access their venom glands. And there's still videos up on the Internet of various people "tickling" slow lorises.

Look for the video with Peter Egan and the "Tickling is torture" campaign. There is a part included that shows this, if you are sensitive you will not want to watch this, as it is a bit heart wrenching.

video_title: Slow Loris is trying to bite
text_content:
A few things about the slow loris, they’re solitary animals. 

The noise it is emitting is actually a hiss. 
When slow lorises are terrified their first reaction is to freeze as a defense. 
When that does not work they will hiss or growl. 
Of that does not work they launch at their attacker. 
The loris in this video as actively trying to bite this person which in accordance to primatologists is its last resort. 

Lorises also have their teeth pulled when they are shipped for captivity as illegal pets. This is so when they do bite they do not transfer their venom to the victim. 

All of this is important information because in short, this slow loris is in Hell and being tortured.

text_content: Fun fact: These animals appear to act like cobras when threatened. They will use 2 methods: If tickled, the slow loris will raise it's arm and the person who is tickling it will instantly be poisened. The other method is that they will gather the poison from their elbows and store it in their teeth. This will allow the slow loris to bite the organism who has threatened it and the organism will immediately be poisened from the bite

video_title: Do SQUIRREL MONKEYS make good PETS?
video_description: Who hasn't dreamt about having a pet monkey? In this video, we look into what it actually takes to have a squirrel monkey as a pet ...
text_content: Knew a family who had a tiny squirrel monkey for almost 30 years.  He slept in his cage but knew how to open the door so essentially ran free in their house.   He was housetrained to use his cage which was lined with newspaper but preferred the cats' litterboxes (they also had up to 3 cats).  The cats initially tried stalking him because he was tiny but he quickly demonstrated that he could put some serious hurt on them if he wanted to.   He could be destructive, and I wouldn't recommend one as a pet to anyone else.  But they had a large family with a lot of kids around to play with him and keep an eye on him.  They had to keep their cupboards locked because he knew how to open them and would tear open cereal boxes and smash peanut butter and jelly jars on the floor if given the opportunity.  He had astonishing speed, leaping ability, and coordination and so 'won' almost any game of tag or keep-away he played with the kids.  He escaped the house a few times and was un-catchable.  During his escapes, he lived in the trees and ate insects and raided the neighbors' bird feeders and any pet food they left outside, but would be back tapping at a window on the first cold night.

video_title: Do SQUIRREL MONKEYS make good PETS?
video_description: Who hasn't dreamt about having a pet monkey? In this video, we look into what it actually takes to have a squirrel monkey as a pet ...
text_content:
I'd prefer that people require training/permits to acquire and raise certain common "exotic" pet species.  
Sadly, with the tragic trend of infant macaques in Cambodia and various parts of Indonesia being abducted and sold, these remarkable species are being exploited, tormented, starved, and abused on film by sadists who appeal to those who prefer to watch this abuse in their free time.  
Monkeys are extremely social and they require more care, attention, and affection than most people are willing (and able) to offer...  
As much as I would love to experience the challenge of raising a monkey from infancy, I also know that my family and my job require the most of my attention.  If I couldn't provide the best possible life for an animal, I would not acquire the animal.  
Pets are cohabitating companions, they are not accessories...

text_content: Leave them in the wild where they belong!  Squirrel monkeys live in very large troops and live a very social squirrel monkey life.  Most people do not make good monkey families, and squirrel monkeys make sweet but challenging pets.  Leave them alone, leave them in the wild to live among their own families.  Taking them as pets denies them a full squirrel monkey life.  It is extremely selfish to put one's own desires for a relationship with a monkey ahead of the monkey's desire to live with its family.

text_content:
THANK YOU for going to her place, and putting high quality info out there! I'm in Canada, we're not allowed to have exotics like monkeys as pets (which is how it should be - dogs & cats have been domesticated , and each side getting advantages - for thousands & thousands & thousands of years!). Monkeys as pets have been maybe the last hundred years (and chimps & orangs, due to the long time their babies are babies, therefore manageable by a human, because they're just babies).
It SHOULD be outlawed. PRIMATES ARE NOT PETS.
PERIOD. 
FULL STOP.
Imagine if we could go buy fully adult humans as pets -- it's insane, but it's the same thing, which means it is equally as insane.
It is cruelty to animals to take a monkey or ape from their family &/or troop. If anyone suggested to these people that they should have a wild bumblebee as a pet ... they would say "LUDICROUS! They need their queen & their hive!!" .... but isn't it the same thing? Why is a bumblebee given the freedom & not a different form of highly intelligent life form. We already know that Orcas belong with their families, and NOT in a tank that is barely the size of a human's kitchen sink by scale (imagine a human trying to fit & live in an "enclosure" the size of a kitchen sink -- it's ridiculous. But the same thing -- especially if these Squirrel & Spider monkey owners are jamming them into a large birdcage. Or large Guinea Pig cage!).

THANK YOU for going & learning about these animals, for learning even how they co-exist in their new troops AND for publicly airing, without giving in to sponsors of whatever company makes "monkey biscuits", because ya just know it's a conglomerate that owns all the usual worldwide brand names!)

Carol

text_content: Monkeys are not meant to be "pets." It is in dogs' DNA to share our lives, but no other animal shares this. We are meant to live in harmony with the earth and her wild things, not to dominate them. A monkey is meant to live in a house just like a human is not meant to live on the ocean floor. Can it be done? Yep. Doesnt mean it SHOULD be done. Allow wild things to stay wild, please. The world is not ours, it belongs to all the wild creatures. We're lucky enough to share it for a tiny amount of time. Not sure where we got the idea this planet is ours but it's time to change our thinking.

video_title: Life of owning a monkey✨❤️🐒 #short #pet #monkey #grwm #beauty #makeup #monkey #baby #kid #animals
text_content: I watch several channels of people with monkeys and their monkeys don't act like that. You let them do what they want and they know they're the ones in control with no repercussions. I don't want to judge you based off a couple minute long videos, but I will say that you might want to be careful letting them do whatever they want like that. Monkeys, even small ones, are dangerous, especially when they believe they are the alpha. Besides that, it seems like every day tasks are difficult because you let them interrupt your tasks and wreak havoc and don't say or do anything. Just be careful and don't let them walk all over you!

video_title: Baby orphan monkeys rescued, kept as pets, causing double trauma and integration nightmare.
video_description: Donald and Adam, two little baby monkeys, rescued a tale of what not to do when you are trying to help and end up causing more ...
text_content:
When you get multiple older babies that have been so humanly socialized do you consider separating them from the start?   They are going to go through the trauma of losing a home they had the run of,  humans they had been with for months and seeing other monkeys for the first time they will have to be separated at some point to stop them from reinforcing the human behaviors between themselves.  Its better to get overvall the trauma at once rather than one at a time, or so we have found. 

 In the US the foreign pet trade is almost non existent, but that has been replace by the monkeymill of breeders who get their hands on a couple of females and a male, usually from an approved breeding facility that is supposed to only breed for research facilities but will often make back door sales when they have extras.  Those facilites are not suppose to take infants from mothers until 8 months or fully weaned but not taking some babies away can reduce the next round of fertile females, so taking infants away from better producers, means they have a few extra infants.  

But whst happens then is we eventually end up with old wirkd monkeys who lineage started in a research breeding facility and then through a backyard breeder.  Sadly, we rarely ever see the parents who were being used to breed as most times they are so mentaly disturbed there is no help for them except euthanasia.  We do see any infants still with the breeder, some young enough that they have not been totally humanized, but their social adaptation can be very much a mix of the different monkeys at the facility, their own parents mental heal issues and if the breeder was handling then a lot.  

New world monkeys are a different story as they are not liked for research. Most ne world monkeys have been being bred in the US for a long time simply because the demand if some species was just yo great to go running around the amazon to trap enough and native cultures never got past spiritual beliefs that kept them for being useful in trapping and selling to a pet trader.  Spider monkeys for example are still popular as they stay relatively small until they get near adulthood and show little aggression before then too.  So rarely, unless found living with people in a state that does not allow monkeys as pets without a rehabilitators license, do we see new world monkeys until older and often we get several at one time from one place.  Old world monkeys do tend to lnow what teeth are for and aggression after about 4-6 months send these into a sanctuary or rehab before they can even be considered for a sanctuary  placement.  Very often backyard breeders will convince a prospective owner they are better off with 2 as they can be monkeys together and not loose their monkeyness, which as they are raised ad pets and humans supply their needs is far from the case.  Then, we get a pair or even 3, that grew up like the two in this video.  Keeping them together under they idea if them being comfort for each other never worked, as they were not only old enough to reject bonding with an afult preferring the bond with each other, but rehab was difficult as they keep reinforcing the behaviors they learned in the first 4-6 months and never fully adapt to trroop society so it always ended up that they needed separation to different troops.  Trying to keep them together for 'comfort' was just delaying the inevitable plus made the rehab and introduction stages more difficult and prolonged.

It may sound harsh to separate them from the start, but actually they start establishing bonds woth others nearer their age and often ones futher through rehap thst have more monkey like social behaviors.

text_content: I'm a new subscriber that's been watching those horrible channels where people adopt "supposedly" orphaned baby monkeys. I'm absolutely appalled at how some of these people treat these little babies coming out of the wild. A few seem to really love them and care for them and actually keep them in their family once they grow into adults. But these "domesticated" monkeys seem to me to be nothing like their wild relatives and wouldn't be able to survive in the wild in a normal monkey troup. Being in a domestic setting truly changes their natures. Another thing I noticed is that babies raised by families that have outdoor spaces to let their pet monkeys roam around in seem much happier than those that are confined to an indoor space. So I'm going to watch your channel regularly now so I can understand better what SHOULD be done with these beautiful babies!!! Thank you for all you do to truly help them!!

I'm sorry, their attitudes just made me so angry, acting like the staff weren't treating the monkeys in their care right.

video_description: Orphan baby monkey Iidiko realises Crumpet is not his true foster mom and jumps into hazels arms The Vervet Forest is a wildlife ...
text_content:
Non-human primates are amazing animals to watch. There are a lot of raw emotions and foster moms, whose hormones, and watching other females claim foster babies, and instincts help them out a lot too, when it comes to their turn to become a “mum” also! 

What also really amazes me are the similarities to humans, in their behaviour and their maternal instincts. If you look at sheep and other livestock and other mammals around the world, in order for a farmer or rescuer and/or caregiver to convince moms whose babies have been stillborn or don’t make it long past birth, they usually rub the birthing and afterbirth fluids from the dead baby onto the orphaned or rejected baby and that is how to facilitate an adoption amongst those animals! 

Monkeys and apes are smarter than that. So it just goes to show that they can be empathetic, have a maternal drive for a baby not belonging to them, by choice and not because they were tricked into it!

And here is the proof as to how the adoption process pan out. When this foster mom took this orphan on, he/she was more than happy to call her mom in every way that was important. So much so, that even unknowingly sitting with another troop member (I’m guessing female as a lot of, probably most monkeys that raise their young communally in a troop, will have aunts and sisters of the infant babysit from time to time, it’s also how sisters of the baby learn skills to become a mother of their own one day!), when the infant realised it, that screech of concern and leap into her mother’s arms is exactly how a baby born to a female would act in that situation! Mom opened her arms and baby leapt into them and only when clinging to mom and embraced by her; does the baby peek out again to see what’s going on ❤

text_content: They are so adorable!! They are so much like human children with that natural curiosity and penchant to explore and experience the world around them..learning about everything by touching and feeling and tasting everything! Though it is sad that there is such a necessity for you to do the work you do and must surely be heartbreaking much of the time (i know it is as i have seen both you and your husband exhibit that heartbreak several times on a few of your videos) but at the same time it must fill your heart with joy and be so awesome to raise these little ones, see their cuteness and the funny little things they do as they explore, to be the one they run to when frightened or unsure of themselves and to experience that look in their face that one can only experience with baby humans and other baby primates...that look of love (and i know many say that does not exist for non human primates but they certainly experience something very close to it if not) and adoration when they look into your eyes and face as they seek guidance in this big world...that you only experience with one when you are having to play substitute mom for them...keep up the awesome work guys!!

text_content: The milk will flow as quickly or as slowly as that cap is tightened.. the looser the quicker because air can enter and release the vacuum created by suckling .. the tighter the slower requiring baby monkey to suckle for only a few sucks at a time due to the high vacuum suction that makes milk flow impossible and tilting the bottle to make sure milk is completely filling the nipple area is so important because baby monkeys expend energy and calories suckling and if they are working hard at trying to get the milk and only getting air they can get pretty bad colicky tummy pain. There are bottles that are bent at the top for just this reason if you need any let me know ... I can actually I'll locate and ship them to you with the smaller nipples .. have a great day guys

text_content: I worked with humans recovering from traumatic brain injuries for a while, and I honestly didn't think this little guy would make it. The tiny size of this infant, "raccoon eyes" with two black eyes, and the dent in his head are extremely bad signs independent of each other, but together there's no way he would've survived in the wild with these injuries without human intervention. He's got at least some brain trauma, and I'm gonna be super surprised and happy if he's ever able to be adopted to live outside a caged environment. It's a great sign if he's taking a bottle. Suckling/swallowing instincts can be lost with head trauma. Such amazing work from the VMF team! Good job, guys!

text_content:
Bushbaby as pets?
The tiny "Nagapie" (night monkey in Afrikaans) are at risk due to humans wanting to keep them as inappropriate pets. 
 
Cute and cuddly looking but did you know they mark their territories by urinating into a cupped hand and then rubbing this on the feet, bushbabies spread their scent wherever they move.  They are also only active at night and need a very specialised diet.
​
Many species of nocturnal primates are increasingly threatened by illegal collecting for the pet trade. When transported outside of their natural range and climate, many bushbabies die. This is partly because they are adapted to a specific climate and landscape, but also because each species has a very specific diet, which they typically do not receive when they are kept as pets, by inexperienced keepers.
 
Furthermore the incorrect diet will result in mal-formed bones, kidney and liver damage, obesity and psychological impairments.
​
Bushbabies are very prone to respiratory problems if the correct temperatures are not maintained.
 
New owners often grow tired quickly; these are nocturnal primates and owners often get bored of them sleeping during the day and do not give enough gum or  adequet insects needed to sustain them at night.  When the novelty wears off the animals are often dumped in the nearest veld. Where they often do not survive as they do not know the landscape; dehydration and starvation.
​
It is AGAINST THE LAW TO KEEP INDIGENOUS WILD ANIMALS AS PETS AND UNETHICAL.
​
If you see someone with an indigenous wild animals as a pet, get in touch.  Or call your local SPCA.

text_content:
Bushbaby as pets?
The tiny "Nagapie" (night monkey in Afrikaans) are at risk due to humans wanting to keep them as inappropriate pets. 
 
Cute and cuddly looking but did you know they mark their territories by urinating into a cupped hand and then rubbing this on the feet, bushbabies spread their scent wherever they move.  They are also only active at night and need a very specialised diet.
​
Many species of nocturnal primates are increasingly threatened by illegal collecting for the pet trade. When transported outside of their natural range and climate, many bushbabies die. This is partly because they are adapted to a specific climate and landscape, but also because each species has a very specific diet, which they typically do not receive when they are kept as pets, by inexperienced keepers.
 
Furthermore the incorrect diet will result in mal-formed bones, kidney and liver damage, obesity and psychological impairments.
​
Bushbabies are very prone to respiratory problems if the correct temperatures are not maintained.
 
New owners often grow tired quickly; these are nocturnal primates and owners often get bored of them sleeping during the day and do not give enough gum or  adequet insects needed to sustain them at night.  When the novelty wears off the animals are often dumped in the nearest veld. Where they often do not survive as they do not know the landscape; dehydration and starvation.
​
It is AGAINST THE LAW TO KEEP INDIGENOUS WILD ANIMALS AS PETS AND UNETHICAL.
​
If you see someone with an indigenous wild animals as a pet, get in touch.  Or call your local SPCA.

text_content: These bush babies are cute but they do not make good pets, especially when they reach puberty. People get them and then realize they mark everything with urine, bite and need a large trive to be mentally stable. They release them ir out them down later because they are difficult to handle. They are very social creatures and live in the wild in large tribes. They are not meant to be domesticated. A baby is cute but they live for many years!

text_content:
I live in Pretoria,  capital of South Africa.We have a family of bush babies living in our garden. We are going to put a nesting box in one of the trees to see if they will move in. A bit of safety as there are a few owls in the area. We put fruit out and a few teaspoons of mixed fruit jam on the trees  now and then. I started planting more indigenous trees to incorouge bird and butterfly life in the garden a few years ago and we are now enjoying the benefits. 
The garden was  saving grace during lockdown.

text_content:
Years ago, kinkajou were sold in pet stores in Washington DC, my mom and dad felt so horrible for these beautiful creatures that they took two of them home and raised them from babies. when I was born to up to two years old, the kinkajou became very protective over me.

Fortunately, a ban on owning them without being a rehabilitation center, my parents who were wildlife rehabilitators, but did not have a rehabilitation center, relocated to the National Smithsonian Zoo in Washington DC where they lived their lives out and as they got older, I got to visit them quite frequently. My parents name is still on a brick near the enclosure.

text_content: Bless your heart for rescuing all these animals. I did what one should never do. I bought an exotic pet without doing any research first because it was a baby and adorable. That pet was an Umbrella or Great White Cockatoo. Fortunately I was willing to put in the work and learn everything I could about them and how to care for them properly. Parrots of all kinds, but especially the different cockatoos, are a lot of work. You can’t just put them in a cage and feed them seeds. If you do you get a very socially unadjusted and sick bird. After all this research and putting into practice what I had learned with my parrot I began to look around and see all the parrots that were being neglected, abused and abandoned because people didn’t realize how much work they were, how long they lived (they are a lifetime pet) and the novelty of owning one had worn off. I now rescue parrots most of whom are a cockatoo of some sort. Cockatoos are very social and needy birds which is why they are the most abused and abandoned parrots out there. You however rescue so many different kinds of animals that require such different and specialized care. My hat is off to you. Well done.

text_content:
Do people not realize how much phones have evolved? You don’t even have to manually dial 911 anymore - you can just press the power and volume buttons together to call for help. That part alone frustrated me.

And then the flat-out stupidity… They walked past the kitchen at least five times and never thought to grab a knife for protection? That made no sense. What really got me was when they woke up and Ben wasn’t there. Instead of being cautious, they just decided to leave the pool without knowing whether he was hiding to trap them or setting something up. Why would you do that?

When they did leave the pool, they didn’t even use that time to come up with a solid plan to save themselves. And the cliff scene? Ben was literally at the edge, can’t even swim, and instead of pulling him into the pool, the guy tried to push him off the cliff - and ended up dying himself. I’m not going to lie, that was the moment I started rooting for Ben.

Am I late to the party or what? And since when do closets lock from the inside? They had multiple chances to take Ben out and failed every single time. Why would you try to physically fight something who’s clearly way stronger than you? And trying to sweet-talk him out of killing people? That completely lost me. I was done at that point.

My uncle owned different kinds of exotic animals for years. Snakes, Crocs, Gators, etc. 

He fostered a chimp one time that some rich asshole kept as a pet that didn’t want it anymore. The chimp was kinda tame, had its own cage/living area etc. 

One day while my uncle was away, the chimp managed to get its cage open. When he came home it had ripped the heads off of 2 pit bulls, 3 cats, and an American alligator. Had piled all the heads in its cage, and was sitting on them like a throne. Idk whatever happened to Capone but animal control came and tranquilized him and took him away.

video_description: This is a little tour of The Realm of the Red Ape at Chester Zoo. It's not far from Chimp Island where Jeff lives. Access is by a long ...
text_content: This is a little tour of The Realm of the Red Ape at Chester Zoo. It's not far from Chimp Island where  Jeff lives. Access is by a long ramp or by a lift. Inside it's very humid, hot, and you will probably get misted and dripped on, but it's worth it. It is home to the Bornean Orangutans and Lar Gibbons, I focused on the orangutans here. The big male is called Willie, he lives with females Sarikei (mum to Tombol and Myrtle) and Leia (mum to Dot and Maria). There's also an old male called Tuan but he's retired from being on show and has his own private quarters, I've seen him once in the end enclosure.

A wonderful surprise today. Valentina, the Pileated Gibbon, has had another baby, born last week I think. The zoo hasn't announced the birth yet, and it's way to early to know if it's a boy or girl (both sexes are born white or buff coloured). 
Barriers have been put around the window to try and keep the public from getting too close. Valentina looked very relaxed and Preda, her older daughter,  was being rather well behaved.
