text_content: Finger monkeys (marmosets) are intensely social creatures. Human companionship cannot replace the need for companionship from another monkey. I know because my friend has 2 and they always let people know you need to get 2 monkeys or they will be depressed and destructive because they want another monkey companion. That's why it's crying a lot. If you had another male companion he would be fine and less clingy.

text_content:
The thing is if you don’t know about macaques you might think this is cruel. But it’s not. That beating you saw that looked awful is very normal in their world. It looks bad but it doesn’t really hurt them. Because when they really want to heart one another it is obvious in the horrid injuries. But thats rear. What looks like beatings is a daily occurrence in the macaque world. It’s how they show and teach each other the order of the hierarchy which is one of the most important things in their world.  
Punch is new so has to be taught about the hierarchy. Also he was hand raised by the keepers until
He was old enough to be with the others. You left that part out. Assimilation with his kind is always what’s best for infant macaques. If he was in the wild and was abandoned by his mother like he was  he would be dead within his first week of life as no other macaque mothers would spare their baby’s milk for him.

text_content:
Omg seriously? 
I'm a HUGE animal advocate and rescuer/foster ect..  you obviously don't know much about monkeys/primates if you're feeding into this.. is this uncomfortable to watch? Yes. Is this tear-jerking, horrific and so sad based on a few seconds? Yes. BUT this is normal behavior for this species. It is how they establish where you fit in in the hierarchy and family. If that adult wanted to truly kill or hurt the baby, it would. This happens all the time in the wild or zoos. It is much better that they are trying to integrate the baby into a social pack rather than continuing isolation. Human emotions aside, it is normal. Doesn't make it not sad, but worse things in troops happen all the time.

video_title: 😫😭all he wants is love😭😫 #babymonkey #punch #animallover #heartbreaking #heartwarming
text_content: The stuffed toy is normal too. Macaques need 24 hours clinging when they are first born, it’s how they feel safe. They normally give them a blanket to cling to when being hand raised by keepers. But sometimes a stuffed toy too. I’ve noticed the stuff toy works better. This is normal for hand raised abandoned infant macaques. This YouTuber obviously doesn’t really know about macaques. Daily beatings is a normal occurrence to affirm hierarchy. And they do it to infants too. Infant macaques are very resilient though. You can’t see it cause this particular breed of macaques are very hairy but infant macaque skin is extremely loose to cope with the way adult macaques including their mothers bite and beat them when they miss behave. It’s all normal guys.

text_content: I do not think you are bad. Just the opposite, i respect everything you have done for these babies, especially Freya. Just because i disagree with you about their diet doesn't mean i think you are a bad caregiver. I just think you are misinformed about one thing. You obviously have Internet, do a Google search or whatever search engine you have over there and find out how bad those bananas are for those babies, especially Freya. Until she heals she needs lots and lots of PROTEIN. Milk has protein and insects, worms and nuts all have protein. She would heal so much better if you feed her what she needs nutritionally. But i think you are a good man. I'm sure this will get deleted as all my other comments do. Anyone who disagree with you gets deleted now? I've said nothing bad about you ever.

text_content: Stop PLEASE taking Frea in your arms systematically as soon as she cries because you're not helping her !! She completely cuts herself off from her fellows and even runs away from them when she should be interacting with them for her own good!! I know it's hard, but you can always give her a hug when she won't ask for it!! She is too dependent on you because at her age, she should already move away from her mother to discover her environment and play with the little ones of her age even if often the mother would hold her by the tail so that she would not stray too far away!! There, Frea is recovering day by day instead of progressing!! Just an opinion!!

video_title: MAKING THE DECISION TO HANDLE FREYA'S BABY MONKEY WAS FULL OF RISK

text_content: At the time, I was one of the first to criticize you for not taking Freya to a vet. How you treated the wounds and how the healing progressed—kudos👍! But there's still a but... Freya will never be able to grasp anything again because her immobile fingers will prevent her from doing so! Everyone knows that macaques like to climb trees, even if they only have one hand, like pirate boss Lucky. But when Freya tries to grab a branch, her stiff fingers will get in the way. I hope I'm wrong about this and that Freya proves me wrong! I wish all the best for her... ❤ 🐒 And for you, young man, keep up the great work. I'm sure without you Freya would have crossed the rainbow bridge long time ago...

text_content:
Hello, I'm commenting as a Japanese person. 
Japanese macaques are known as apes that live in the northernmost cold regions of the world, mainly in scrub forests and rocky mountains. They live in groups, constantly moving through the forests, eating mulberry, oak, chestnut and other fruits. They are very active, and when there is no food in the forest, they all come down to the villages and attack crops in the fields and people, destroying persimmons and other fruits. There are many comments from viewers saying they want to adopt Punchi as a pet, but they are always happiest living in a group, and are by no means animals that are suited to being kept as pets. Also, during the breeding season, their entire faces and rears turn bright red, and they do not look very clean, so they are not creatures that can be easily controlled by humans, and I think they are best suited to living in nature or in an enclosure ♪ 
Punch-kun will have experienced many challenges there and will surely one day grow into a strong leader of that group. 😉

video_title: 29 January 2026
text_content:
Just for general information, the video starts off with the mother jamming her fingers into her infant's mouth to remove some item of food it has stored inside the pouch located in its throat because she wants it for herself.  🥴

While the second part shows her in the process of training her pup to leave her teats alone in order to teach it when it may nurse - otherwise it will get physically reprimanded, sometimes to the point of being beaten, which is why we see so many videos where an infant will approach its mother in fear of being beaten for any number of reasons she might have that day. 🤕

And the third scene, (?), she is actually in the process of teaching her pup to walk own its own in order to discourage it from riding on her back everywhere they go.

Tough Life being a Macaque infant since it takes awhile for them to realize what is needed from them by their mother.

I hope this helped answer a few questions as to what was going on. 🤔 😀 👍

text_content:
I totally understand why one would remove the more independant baby when you have a very clingy one  but what I dont understand is returning the independant one back into the same enviroment it was removed from hoping the clingy ones bond with a foster will shift.  Of course, you have had decent luck using this behavior modification but it seems to be odd to modify a behavior to such an extent when the idea is to return the primates to as close to a wild state as possible.  

In the wild baby to baby clinging/comfort would rarely be toletated by a mom except in a rare case where related mlms might allomother for a short time so a closely related female can get a break.  Even then you will see the allomother dispense with negative reactions if one baby demonstrated behavior such as what you call 'back packing'.

Allowing a more independant baby back in the same enviroment after a few days and what seems like decent boding is giving the more independant baby the message that they are not primarily worth a mother as clingy friend now has a total relationship with a mom and can feel rejected.  Or the clingy baby can relapse and separate its bond somewhat so that neither baby has the bong they should have. Plus its also teaching females that while they may feel an urge, which is correct and natural, to separate  the babies, that they should see behavior as natural.  Of course, in a sanctuary learned behavior that is not normal may not be a problem, unless enough genetations pass that the instinct gets buried and is problematic if there comes a release situation.

Its great to have jnfants develop family and troop bonds which normally happens as they grow, socialize under mom's guidence, play, explore etc with it each other, but babies bonding with other babies, during periods they are in human care is very abnormal.  

Removing an independant baby to allow a foster mom to adjust that behavior is good but its not good that those babies be back together, even if grafted to other females, until release into troop where the troop will enforce the mother baby bond and not allow abnormal baby to baby boding, or better yet switching the enviroment so independant baby is moved to a different troop altogether.  I dont really understand the reason for having two or more babies that have established abnormal bonding to be allowed back to establish that pattern to any extent again, often a pattern not well observed by monitors, when the population is large enough to accomodate breaking up that behavior forever.  I understand it may seem kind to human care givers when babies make what seem to be loving, supporting, pairings and see it as allowing them extra comfort but in truth, this is not how primates bond and they need to bond through socialization, rank, behavior

video_title: How Raccoons (Almost) Got Away With It
video_description: Nessie's Linktree- https://linktr.ee/Lochnessofficial TikTok: https://www.tiktok.com/@lochnessofficial?_r=1&_t=ZT-93hL3Ntn1mY ...
text_content:
EDITED TO ADD
I wasn’t expecting so many reactions, so could you do me a favor and read through the thread before asking questions or making comments to see if yours has already been answered? I’m repeating myself a lot. 

Exotic mammals veterinarian here. I treated pet and wild raccoons for three decades and I’m here to tell you the babies are super-cute and friendly. BUT…once they reach adulthood, most of them just want you dead. I saw more owners get bitten by raccoons than any other pet. You have to sedate them to do literally anything to them, even brush them.

They’re smart, they’re cute, they’re funny—all those things—as long as they get to do anything they want. If you try to stop them from eating your kitchen cabinets or shredding your mattress, it’s over. Not to mention that yes, they wash their food in bowls. But, what most people don’t know is that they also poop in those bowls. 

They make terrible pets. Enjoy them in the wild and leave them alone.

I’ll also add this: 

Raccoons are one of the Big Four rabies vector species. In most of Europe, they’re considered such a dangerous invasive species that they can be hunted, trapped, or shot on sight. In most U.S. states, you need a special permit to own one. 

They also have a high incidence of what’s known as the “raccoon roundworm,” which can be transmitted to dogs and people. In people, it invades the brain and can cause all kinds of neurological symptoms, including death. Children are at most risk, because transmission is from fecal contamination of soil, i.e., putting dirty fingers in mouths. 

Fortunately, human deaths from raccoon rabies or roundworm are rare, but please don’t push it by trying to make one a pet.

text_content: Honestly, the fact that these guys have hands alone immediately shuts down any thoughts of keeping one as a pet for me. Dogs already get into things they absolutely should not be able to; and giving them both a proclivity to climb, opposable thumbs, and the intelligence to use them effectively would render essentially nothing in your house safe from them.

video_title: How Raccoons (Almost) Got Away With It
video_description: Nessie's Linktree- https://linktr.ee/Lochnessofficial TikTok: https://www.tiktok.com/@lochnessofficial?_r=1&_t=ZT-93hL3Ntn1mY ...
text_content:
Camping in northern NJ in the late '90's; me and two friends woke up to raccoons... robbing us. Not stealing from us; robbing us. There was one raccoon for each of us, plus the one going through our food. 
They stood on our chests and bared their teeth, growling directly into our faces while the one going through the cooler stole beer, potato chips and a bag of hot dogs. 
They backed out of the tents and all three of us swore we could practically HEAR them telling us to count to 100 before we started breathing again. 
I'm still not sure how I feel about it.

text_content: Raccoons are scary smart, and I'm convinced theyre smarter than we realize. I used to work nights, so Id be up at all hours even on my days off. I once had a some late-night movie watching interrupted by what sounded like a cat trying to imitate whalesong coming from my carport. I poked my head out a window to see about a dozen raccoons of various sizes *sitting* (on their haunches) *in a perfect circle* while one large raccoon stood i  the center of the circle,  "talking" and gesticulating wildly. This went on for minutes, then suddenly the "talking" one dropped to all fours, quickly followed by the rest, and then they instantly scattered into the darkness. At the time I lived two hours south of Toronto, so same stock as the UberCoons of t-dot

text_content: Fun fact, raccoons are smart enough to "hire" babysitters. When I was little this raccoon was coming by nearly daily. We never directly fed her but she'd help herself to cat food. After a while she came by with her three babies. Then the next day she brought her babies up on our deck while my parents were out there drinking their morning coffees, chittered at her babies to stay, then went off to forge without her kids in tow. Came back like an hour later picked up her kids and left. After roughly a week of this, dad went to pet one of the babies and momma raccoon grabbed his hand in her mouth and lightly bit. Not enough to draw blood, but enough to make sure dad got the message of "I trust you to watch them, do not touch them." She kept bringing her kids until they were bigger. I assume big enough they could be trusted to forge with her without issues. It's not very common, but I have heard similar stories from other people as well.

text_content: I was sitting in a deer stand one evening when a family of raccoons showed up.  There was a feeder in a nearby tree hung from a limb by a rope.  This feeder was a big can with a motorized device set to disperse corn at only certain times of the day.  The mother raccoon climbed the tree, walked down the limb and slid down the rope to the top of the feeder.  She then proceeded to swing the feeder Tarzan style until it banged against the trunk of the tree multiple times, spilling corn from the feeder each time.  After she'd gotten enough to feed her family, she reversed the process and they all dined on a huge pile of corn.

text_content:
Mule deer have also adapted to life in cities in Canada. The does spend almost all of their time in rural towns since there’s a lot of food and bears, cougars, and coyotes tend to stay away or get dealt with. This has lead to generations of mule deer growing up in and living around humans, to the point where we know when to be on our toes when walking our dogs. In Spring, does become very protective of their fawns when non-deer quadrupeds are involved and will step up if you get too close. October-November is when the bucks roll in when they’re in rut, so you either steer clear or carry a stick (or have a mean right hook) to save yourself or your dog an expensive lesson.

I swear, I’ve seen these deer waiting for a red light before crossing the street, they’re that habituated.

text_content: As someone who's absolutely fascinated with raccoons, I enjoyed this video, and also want to add something noteworthy! First Lady to President Coolridge had a pet raccoon named Rebecca. Raccoons -have- successfully been pets before, however, just because it's been done, doesn't mean it's a good idea. Unless you're an expert on them, besides any possible legal issues, you'd have to do your best to provide immunization with whatever options your local pet doc has, provide massive amounts of training, be prepared for massive amounts of training to likely fail, and have a lot of spare money as well as patience. As much as I love raccoons, and am very passionate about them, just because they have proven tameable before does not mean it is in your best interest to do so. Just admire them as part of nature.
